text.title: GitOps — Core Principles Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules including a `skip-this-methodology` rule. Each pairs a binary check with sourced rationale.

rule.statement: All cluster-affecting changes MUST flow through Git. `kubectl apply` / UI changes by humans are rejected; emergency overrides require a documented break-glass procedure.
rule.rationale: Out-of-band changes destroy audit + reproducibility; the value of GitOps is exactly this discipline.

rule.statement: Cluster state MUST be declarative — desired-state YAML, never imperative scripts. `kubectl run`, ad-hoc Jobs, manual ConfigMap edits are rejected.
rule.rationale: Imperative state can't be diffed or reconciled; declarative is the only auditable shape.

rule.statement: An operator (ArgoCD / Flux) MUST continuously reconcile cluster state against Git within a bounded interval (default ≤3 minutes). Manual sync triggers are an audit hint, not the primary mechanism.
rule.rationale: Drift accumulates between manual syncs; bounded reconciliation catches drift quickly.

rule.statement: Reconciliation status (synced / out-of-sync / health) MUST be exposed as metrics + dashboard. Silent drift is rejected.
rule.rationale: Drift you can't see is drift you don't fix; metrics make GitOps observable.

rule.statement: Tool choice (ArgoCD vs Flux) MUST be documented with rationale: ArgoCD for UI-driven multi-tenant orgs, Flux for CRD-native automation-heavy orgs. Cargo-cult choice is rejected.
rule.rationale: Team-fit drives long-run success; the tool wars don't matter compared to operational fit.

rule.statement: Skip when (a) non-Kubernetes platform, (b) team workflow centred on imperative kubectl, or (c) single-dev cluster with no audit need.
rule.rationale: GitOps benefits are conditional on Kubernetes + declarative discipline + audit need; without them it's ceremony.
